terms.definition: Derived from the root *-ntu* (person) and the prefix *ba-* (people). It literally means 'the people' and refers to a vast family of over 500 related languages in sub-Saharan Africa.
The reconstructed ancestral language of all modern Bantu languages. Its vocabulary suggests a highland savanna homeland, containing words for sorghum and cattle but not rainforest crops.
A grammatical system characteristic of Bantu languages, where nouns are grouped into categories (e.g., humans, animals) marked by specific prefixes that dictate agreement throughout a sentence.
An early Iron Age archaeological culture in the Great Lakes region known for distinctive dimple-based pottery and sophisticated iron-smelting furnaces.
Meaning 'between the lakes,' referring to the fertile, high-altitude region in East Africa surrounding the Great Lakes (Victoria, Tanganyika, etc.), a key staging ground for migrations.
A composite term for the indigenous hunter-gatherer (San) and pastoralist (Khoikhoi) peoples of Southern Africa, characterized by deep genetic diversity and unique 'click' languages.
A supreme creator deity recognized in many traditional African religions. Often viewed as remote, this belief reflects a devolutionary memory of the original monotheism of Noah.
